question:
Let $S_n$ denote the set of all permutations of the numbers $1,2,\dots,n$.
For $\pi \in S_n$, let $\sigma(\pi) = 1$ if $\pi$ is an even permutation
and $\sigma(\pi) = -1$ if $\pi$ is an odd permutation.
Also, let $\nu(\pi)$ denote the number of fixed points of $\pi$.
Show that
\[
\sum_{\pi \in S_n} \frac{\sigma(\pi)}{\nu(\pi) + 1} = (-1)^{n+1}
\frac{n}{n+1}.
\]
\end{itemize}
\end{document}
solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
Let $I$ be the identity matrix, and let
$J_x$ be the matrix with $x$'s on the diagonal and 1's elsewhere.
Note that $J_x - (x-1)I$, being the all 1's matrix, has rank 1 and trace $n$,
so has $n-1$ eigenvalues equal to 0 and one equal to $n$.
Hence $J_x$ has $n-1$ eigenvalues equal to $x-1$ and one equal to $x+n-1$,
implying
\[
\det J_x = (x+n-1)(x-1)^{n-1}.
\]
On the other hand, we may expand the determinant as a sum indexed by
permutations, in which case we get
\[
\det J_x = \sum_{\pi \in S_n} \sgn(\pi) x^{\nu(\pi)}.
\]
Integrating both sides from 0 to 1 (and substituting $y=1-x$) yields
\begin{align*}
\sum_{\pi \in S_n} \frac{\sgn(\pi)}{\nu(\pi) + 1}
&= \int_0^1 (x+n-1)(x-1)^{n-1}\,dx \\
&= \int_0^1 (-1)^{n+1} (n-y)y^{n-1}\,dy \\
&= (-1)^{n+1} \frac{n}{n+1},
\end{align*}
as desired.

\textbf{Second solution:}
We start by recalling a form of the principle of inclusion-exclusion:
if $f$ is a function on the power set of $\{1, \dots, n\}$, then
\[
f(S) = \sum_{T \supseteq S} (-1)^{|T|-|S|} \sum_{U \supseteq T} f(U).
\]
In this case we take $f(S)$ to be the sum of $\sigma(\pi)$
over all permutations $\pi$ whose fixed points are exactly $S$.
Then $\sum_{U \supseteq T} f(U) = 1$ if $|T| \geq n-1$
and 0 otherwise (since a permutation group on 2 or more symbols has as many even and odd permutations), so
\[
f(S) = (-1)^{n-|S|}(1 - n + |S|).
\]
The desired sum can thus be written, by grouping over fixed point sets, as
\begin{multline*}
\sum_{i=0}^n \binom{n}{i} (-1)^{n-i} \frac{1-n+i}{i+1} \\
\begin{aligned}
&= \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^{n-i} \binom{n}{i} - \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^{n-i} \frac{n}{i+1}
\binom{n}{i} \\
&= 0 - \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^{n-i} \frac{n}{n+1} \binom{n+1}{i+1} \\
&= (-1)^{n+1} \frac{n}{n+1}.
\end{aligned}
\end{multline*}

\textbf{Third solution:}
(by Richard Stanley)
The \emph{cycle indicator} of the symmetric group $S_n$ is defined by
\[
Z_n(x_1, \dots, x_n) = \sum_{\pi \in S_n} x_1^{c_1(\pi)} \cdots x_n^{c_n(\pi)},
\]
where $c_i(\pi)$ is the number of cycles of $\pi$ of length $i$.
Put
\[
F_n = \sum_{\pi \in S_n} \sigma(\pi) x^{\nu(\pi)} =
Z_n(x,-1,1,-1,1,\dots)
\]
and
\[
f(n) =  \sum_{\pi \in S_n} \frac{\sigma(\pi)}{\nu(\pi) + 1}
= \int_0^1 F_n(x)\,dx.
\]
A standard argument in enumerative combinatorics (the
Exponential Formula) gives
\[
\sum_{n=0}^\infty Z_n(x_1,\dots,x_n) \frac{t^n}{n!}
= \exp \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} x_k \frac{t^k}{k},
\]
yielding
\begin{align*}
\sum_{n=0}^\infty
f(n) \frac{t^n}{n!} &= \int_0^1 \exp \left( xt - \frac{t^2}{2}
+ \frac{t^3}{3} - \cdots \right)\,dx \\
&= \int_0^1 e^{(x-1)t + \log(1+t)}\,dx \\
&= \int_0^1 (1+t) e^{(x-1)t}\,dx \\
&= \frac{1}{t} (1-e^{-t}) (1+t).
\end{align*}
Expanding the right side as a Taylor series and comparing coefficients
yields the desired result.

\textbf{Fourth solution (sketch):}
(by David Savitt)
We prove the identity of rational functions
\[
\sum_{\pi \in S_n} \frac{\sigma(\pi)}{\nu(\pi) + x}
= \frac{(-1)^{n+1} n! (x+n-1)}{x(x+1)\cdots(x+n)}
\]
by induction on $n$, which for $x=1$ implies the desired result.
(This can also be deduced as in the other solutions, but in this argument
it is necessary to formulate the strong induction hypothesis.)

Let $R(n,x)$ be the right hand side of the above equation. It is
easy to verify that
\begin{align*}
R(x,n) &= R(x+1,n-1) + (n-1)! \frac{(-1)^{n+1}}{x} \\
&\qquad + \sum_{l=2}^{n-1} (-1)^{l-1}  \frac{(n-1)!}{(n-l)!} R(x,n-l),
\end{align*}
since the sum telescopes. To prove the desired equality,
it suffices to show that the left hand side satisfies the same
recurrence. This follows because we can classify each $\pi \in S_n$
as either fixing $n$, being an $n$-cycle, or having $n$ in an
$l$-cycle for one of $l=2,\dots,n-1$; writing the sum
over these classes gives the desired recurrence.

\end{itemize}


\end{document}

descriptive_long.question:
Problem:
Let $permset$ denote the set of all permutations of the numbers $1,2,\dots,sizeparam$.
For $permvar \in permset$, let $parities(permvar) = 1$ if $permvar$ is an even permutation
and $parities(permvar) = -1$ if $permvar$ is an odd permutation.
Also, let $fixedcount(permvar)$ denote the number of fixed points of $permvar$.
Show that
\[
\sum_{permvar \in permset} \frac{parities(permvar)}{fixedcount(permvar) + 1} = (-1)^{sizeparam+1}
\frac{sizeparam}{sizeparam+1}.
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
Let $identity$ be the identity matrix, and let
$onesmatrix$ be the matrix with $variablex$'s on the diagonal and 1's elsewhere.
Note that $onesmatrix - (variablex-1)identity$, being the all 1's matrix, has rank 1 and trace $sizeparam$,
so has $sizeparam-1$ eigenvalues equal to 0 and one equal to $sizeparam$.
Hence $onesmatrix$ has $sizeparam-1$ eigenvalues equal to $variablex-1$ and one equal to $variablex+sizeparam-1$,
implying
\[
\det onesmatrix = (variablex+sizeparam-1)(variablex-1)^{sizeparam-1}.
\]
On the other hand, we may expand the determinant as a sum indexed by
permutations, in which case we get
\[
\det onesmatrix = \sum_{permvar \in permset} \sgn(permvar) \, variablex^{fixedcount(permvar)}.
\]
Integrating both sides from 0 to 1 (and substituting $variabley=1-variablex$) yields
\begin{align*}
\sum_{permvar \in permset} \frac{\sgn(permvar)}{fixedcount(permvar) + 1}
&= \int_0^1 (variablex+sizeparam-1)(variablex-1)^{sizeparam-1}\,dvariablex \\
&= \int_0^1 (-1)^{sizeparam+1} (sizeparam-variabley)\, variabley^{sizeparam-1}\,dvariabley \\
&= (-1)^{sizeparam+1} \frac{sizeparam}{sizeparam+1},
\end{align*}
as desired.

\textbf{Second solution:}
We start by recalling a form of the principle of inclusion-exclusion:
if $functionf$ is a function on the power set of $\{1, \dots, sizeparam\}$, then
\[
functionf(setvar) = \sum_{subsett \supseteq setvar} (-1)^{|subsett|-|setvar|}
\sum_{subsetu \supseteq subsett} functionf(subsetu).
\]
In this case we take $functionf(setvar)$ to be the sum of $parities(permvar)$
over all permutations $permvar$ whose fixed points are exactly $setvar$.
Then $\sum_{subsetu \supseteq subsett} functionf(subsetu) = 1$ if $|subsett| \geq sizeparam-1$
and 0 otherwise (since a permutation group on 2 or more symbols has as many even and odd permutations), so
\[
functionf(setvar) = (-1)^{sizeparam-|setvar|}(1 - sizeparam + |setvar|).
\]
The desired sum can thus be written, by grouping over fixed-point sets, as
\begin{multline*}
\sum_{indexi=0}^{sizeparam} \binom{sizeparam}{indexi} (-1)^{sizeparam-indexi} \frac{1-sizeparam+indexi}{indexi+1} \\
\begin{aligned}
&= \sum_{indexi=0}^{sizeparam} (-1)^{sizeparam-indexi} \binom{sizeparam}{indexi}
- \sum_{indexi=0}^{sizeparam} (-1)^{sizeparam-indexi} \frac{sizeparam}{indexi+1}
\binom{sizeparam}{indexi} \\
&= 0 - \sum_{indexi=0}^{sizeparam} (-1)^{sizeparam-indexi} \frac{sizeparam}{sizeparam+1}
\binom{sizeparam+1}{indexi+1} \\
&= (-1)^{sizeparam+1} \frac{sizeparam}{sizeparam+1}.
\end{aligned}
\end{multline*}

\textbf{Third solution:}
(by Richard Stanley)
The \emph{cycle indicator} of the symmetric group $S_{sizeparam}$ is defined by
\[
cycleindicator(variableone, \dots, variablen) = \sum_{permvar \in permset}
variableone^{cyclecount(permvar)} \cdots variablen^{cyclecount(permvar)},
\]
where $cyclecount(permvar)$ is the number of cycles of $permvar$ of length $indexi$.
Put
\[
generating = \sum_{permvar \in permset} parities(permvar)\, variablex^{fixedcount(permvar)}
= cycleindicator(variablex,-1,1,-1,1,\dots)
\]
and
\[
functionf(sizeparam) =  \sum_{permvar \in permset} \frac{parities(permvar)}{fixedcount(permvar) + 1}
= \int_0^1 generating\,dvariablex.
\]
A standard argument in enumerative combinatorics (the
Exponential Formula) gives
\[
\sum_{sizeparam=0}^\infty cycleindicator(variableone,\dots,variablen)
\frac{variablet^{sizeparam}}{sizeparam!}
= \exp \sum_{indexk=1}^{\infty} variablek \frac{variablet^{indexk}}{indexk},
\]
yielding
\begin{align*}
\sum_{sizeparam=0}^\infty
functionf(sizeparam) \frac{variablet^{sizeparam}}{sizeparam!} &=
\int_0^1 \exp \left( variablex\,variablet - \frac{variablet^2}{2}
+ \frac{variablet^3}{3} - \cdots \right)\,dvariablex \\
&= \int_0^1 e^{(variablex-1)variablet + \log(1+variablet)}\,dvariablex \\
&= \int_0^1 (1+variablet) e^{(variablex-1)variablet}\,dvariablex \\
&= \frac{1}{variablet} \bigl(1-e^{-variablet}\bigr) (1+variablet).
\end{align*}
Expanding the right side as a Taylor series and comparing coefficients
yields the desired result.

\textbf{Fourth solution (sketch):}
(by David Savitt)
We prove the identity of rational functions
\[
\sum_{permvar \in permset} \frac{parities(permvar)}{fixedcount(permvar) + variablex}
= \frac{(-1)^{sizeparam+1} sizeparam! (variablex+sizeparam-1)}{variablex(variablex+1)\cdots(variablex+sizeparam)}
\]
by induction on $sizeparam$, which for $variablex=1$ implies the desired result.
(This can also be deduced as in the other solutions, but in this argument
it is necessary to formulate the strong induction hypothesis.)

Let $R(sizeparam,variablex)$ be the right-hand side of the above equation. It is
easy to verify that
\begin{align*}
R(variablex,sizeparam) &= R(variablex+1,sizeparam-1) + (sizeparam-1)!
\frac{(-1)^{sizeparam+1}}{variablex} \\
&\qquad + \sum_{l=2}^{sizeparam-1} (-1)^{l-1}  \frac{(sizeparam-1)!}{(sizeparam-l)!}
R(variablex,sizeparam-l),
\end{align*}
since the sum telescopes. To prove the desired equality,
it suffices to show that the left-hand side satisfies the same
recurrence. This follows because we can classify each $permvar \in permset$
as either fixing $sizeparam$, being a $sizeparam$-cycle, or having $sizeparam$ in an
$l$-cycle for one of $l=2,\dots,sizeparam-1$; writing the sum
over these classes gives the desired recurrence.

\end{itemize}

\end{document}

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $windmilltoynum$ denote the set of all permutations of the numbers $1,2,\dots,goldthread$.
For $\pi \in windmilltoynum$, let $boardwalk(\pi) = 1$ if $\pi$ is an even permutation
and $boardwalk(\pi) = -1$ if $\pi$ is an odd permutation.
Also, let $tailspin(\pi)$ denote the number of fixed points of $\pi$.
Show that
\[
\sum_{\pi \in windmilltoynum} \frac{boardwalk(\pi)}{tailspin(\pi) + 1} = (-1)^{goldthread+1}
\frac{goldthread}{goldthread+1}.
\]
\end{itemize}
\end{document}
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
Let $spaceship$ be the identity matrix, and let
$distillery$ be the matrix with lemonjuice's on the diagonal and 1's elsewhere.
Note that $distillery - (lemonjuice-1)spaceship$, being the all 1's matrix, has rank 1 and trace goldthread,
so has goldthread-1 eigenvalues equal to 0 and one equal to goldthread.
Hence $distillery$ has goldthread-1 eigenvalues equal to lemonjuice-1 and one equal to lemonjuice+goldthread-1,
implying
\[
\det distillery = (lemonjuice+goldthread-1)(lemonjuice-1)^{goldthread-1}.
\]
On the other hand, we may expand the determinant as a sum indexed by
permutations, in which case we get
\[
\det distillery = \sum_{\pi \in windmilltoynum} \sgn(\pi) lemonjuice^{tailspin(\pi)}.
\]
Integrating both sides from 0 to 1 (and substituting raincloud=1-lemonjuice) yields
\begin{align*}
\sum_{\pi \in windmilltoynum} \frac{\sgn(\pi)}{tailspin(\pi) + 1}
&= \int_0^1 (lemonjuice+goldthread-1)(lemonjuice-1)^{goldthread-1}\,dlemonjuice \\
&= \int_0^1 (-1)^{goldthread+1} (goldthread-raincloud)raincloud^{goldthread-1}\,draincloud \\
&= (-1)^{goldthread+1} \frac{goldthread}{goldthread+1},
\end{align*}
as desired.

\textbf{Second solution:}
We start by recalling a form of the principle of inclusion-exclusion:
if $dragonfly$ is a function on the power set of $\{1, \dots, goldthread\}$, then
\[
dragonfly(windmilltoy) = \sum_{riverbank \supseteq windmilltoy} (-1)^{|riverbank|-|windmilltoy|} \sum_{arrowshaft \supseteq riverbank} dragonfly(arrowshaft).
\]
In this case we take $dragonfly(windmilltoy)$ to be the sum of $boardwalk(\pi)$
over all permutations $\pi$ whose fixed points are exactly $windmilltoy$.
Then $\sum_{arrowshaft \supseteq riverbank} dragonfly(arrowshaft) = 1$ if $|riverbank| \geq goldthread-1$
and 0 otherwise (since a permutation group on 2 or more symbols has as many even and odd permutations), so
\[
dragonfly(windmilltoy) = (-1)^{goldthread-|windmilltoy|}(1 - goldthread + |windmilltoy|).
\]
The desired sum can thus be written, by grouping over fixed point sets, as
\begin{multline*}
\sum_{earthworm=0}^{goldthread} \binom{goldthread}{earthworm} (-1)^{goldthread-earthworm} \frac{1-goldthread+earthworm}{earthworm+1} \\
\begin{aligned}
&= \sum_{earthworm=0}^{goldthread} (-1)^{goldthread-earthworm} \binom{goldthread}{earthworm} - \sum_{earthworm=0}^{goldthread} (-1)^{goldthread-earthworm} \frac{goldthread}{earthworm+1}
\binom{goldthread}{earthworm} \\
&= 0 - \sum_{earthworm=0}^{goldthread} (-1)^{goldthread-earthworm} \frac{goldthread}{goldthread+1} \binom{goldthread+1}{earthworm+1} \\
&= (-1)^{goldthread+1} \frac{goldthread}{goldthread+1}.
\end{aligned}
\end{multline*}

\textbf{Third solution:}
(by Richard Stanley)
The \emph{cycle indicator} of the symmetric group $windmilltoynum$ is defined by
\[
snowglobe(sugarcane, \dots, jaguarundi) = \sum_{\pi \in windmilltoynum} sugarcane^{cornflower(\pi)} \cdots jaguarundi^{cornflower(\pi)},
\]
where $cornflower(\pi)$ is the number of cycles of $\pi$ of length $i$.
Put
\[
mousetrap = \sum_{\pi \in windmilltoynum} boardwalk(\pi) lemonjuice^{tailspin(\pi)} =
snowglobe(lemonjuice,-1,1,-1,1,\dots)
\]
and
\[
dragonfly(goldthread) =  \sum_{\pi \in windmilltoynum} \frac{boardwalk(\pi)}{tailspin(\pi) + 1}
= \int_0^1 mousetrap\,dlemonjuice.
\]
A standard argument in enumerative combinatorics (the
Exponential Formula) gives
\[
\sum_{goldthread=0}^\infty snowglobe(sugarcane,\dots,jaguarundi) \frac{bluewhale^{goldthread}}{goldthread!}
= \exp \sum_{scarecrow=1}^{\infty} buttercup \frac{bluewhale^{scarecrow}}{scarecrow},
\]
yielding
\begin{align*}
\sum_{goldthread=0}^\infty
dragonfly(goldthread) \frac{bluewhale^{goldthread}}{goldthread!} &= \int_0^1 \exp \left( lemonjuice bluewhale - \frac{bluewhale^2}{2}
+ \frac{bluewhale^3}{3} - \cdots \right)\,dlemonjuice \\
&= \int_0^1 e^{(lemonjuice-1)bluewhale + \log(1+bluewhale)}\,dlemonjuice \\
&= \int_0^1 (1+bluewhale) e^{(lemonjuice-1)bluewhale}\,dlemonjuice \\
&= \frac{1}{bluewhale} (1-e^{-bluewhale}) (1+bluewhale).
\end{align*}
Expanding the right side as a Taylor series and comparing coefficients
yields the desired result.

\textbf{Fourth solution (sketch):}
(by David Savitt)
We prove the identity of rational functions
\[
\sum_{\pi \in windmilltoynum} \frac{boardwalk(\pi)}{tailspin(\pi) + lemonjuice}
= \frac{(-1)^{goldthread+1} goldthread! (lemonjuice+goldthread-1)}{lemonjuice(lemonjuice+1)\cdots(lemonjuice+goldthread)}
\]
by induction on goldthread, which for lemonjuice=1 implies the desired result.
(This can also be deduced as in the other solutions, but in this argument
it is necessary to formulate the strong induction hypothesis.)

Let $R(goldthread,lemonjuice)$ be the right hand side of the above equation. It is
easy to verify that
\begin{align*}
R(lemonjuice,goldthread) &= R(lemonjuice+1,goldthread-1) + (goldthread-1)! \frac{(-1)^{goldthread+1}}{lemonjuice} \\
&\qquad + \sum_{l=2}^{goldthread-1} (-1)^{l-1}  \frac{(goldthread-1)!}{(goldthread-l)!} R(lemonjuice,goldthread-l),
\end{align*}
since the sum telescopes. To prove the desired equality,
it suffices to show that the left hand side satisfies the same
recurrence. This follows because we can classify each $\pi \in windmilltoynum$
as either fixing goldthread, being an goldthread-cycle, or having goldthread in an
$l$-cycle for one of $l=2,\dots,goldthread-1$; writing the sum
over these classes gives the desired recurrence.

\end{itemize}

\end{document}

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $singularity$ denote the set of all permutations of the numbers $1,2,\dots,nullvalue$.
For $staticmap \in singularity$, let $magnitude(staticmap) = 1$ if $staticmap$ is an even permutation
and $magnitude(staticmap) = -1$ if $staticmap$ is an odd permutation.
Also, let $mobility(staticmap)$ denote the number of fixed points of $staticmap$.
Show that
\[
\sum_{staticmap \in singularity} \frac{magnitude(staticmap)}{mobility(staticmap) + 1} = (-1)^{nullvalue+1}
\frac{nullvalue}{nullvalue+1}.
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
Let $difference$ be the identity matrix, and let
$emptymatrix$ be the matrix with $constant$'s on the diagonal and 1's elsewhere.
Note that $emptymatrix - (constant-1)difference$, being the all 1's matrix, has rank 1 and trace $nullvalue$,
so has $nullvalue-1$ eigenvalues equal to 0 and one equal to $nullvalue$.
Hence $emptymatrix$ has $nullvalue-1$ eigenvalues equal to $constant-1$ and one equal to $constant+nullvalue-1$,
implying
\[
\det emptymatrix = (constant+nullvalue-1)(constant-1)^{nullvalue-1}.
\]
On the other hand, we may expand the determinant as a sum indexed by
permutations, in which case we get
\[
\det emptymatrix = \sum_{staticmap \in singularity} \sgn(staticmap) constant^{mobility(staticmap)}.
\]
Integrating both sides from 0 to 1 (and substituting $steadyval=1-constant$) yields
\begin{align*}
\sum_{staticmap \in singularity} \frac{\sgn(staticmap)}{mobility(staticmap) + 1}
&= \int_0^1 (constant+nullvalue-1)(constant-1)^{nullvalue-1}\,dconstant \\
&= \int_0^1 (-1)^{nullvalue+1} (nullvalue-steadyval)steadyval^{nullvalue-1}\,dsteadyval \\
&= (-1)^{nullvalue+1} \frac{nullvalue}{nullvalue+1},
\end{align*}
as desired.

\textbf{Second solution:}
We start by recalling a form of the principle of inclusion-exclusion:
if $variable$ is a function on the power set of $\{1, \dots, nullvalue\}$, then
\[
variable(emptiness) = \sum_{universalset \supseteq emptiness} (-1)^{|universalset|-|emptiness|} \sum_{emptyset \supseteq universalset} variable(emptyset).
\]
In this case we take $variable(emptiness)$ to be the sum of $magnitude(staticmap)$
over all permutations $staticmap$ whose fixed points are exactly $emptiness$.
Then $\sum_{emptyset \supseteq universalset} variable(emptyset) = 1$ if $|universalset| \geq nullvalue-1$
and 0 otherwise (since a permutation group on 2 or more symbols has as many even and odd permutations), so
\[
variable(emptiness) = (-1)^{nullvalue-|emptiness|}(1 - nullvalue + |emptiness|).
\]
The desired sum can thus be written, by grouping over fixed point sets, as
\begin{multline*}
\sum_{aggregate=0}^{nullvalue} \binom{nullvalue}{aggregate} (-1)^{nullvalue-aggregate} \frac{1-nullvalue+aggregate}{aggregate+1} \\
\begin{aligned}
&= \sum_{aggregate=0}^{nullvalue} (-1)^{nullvalue-aggregate} \binom{nullvalue}{aggregate} - \sum_{aggregate=0}^{nullvalue} (-1)^{nullvalue-aggregate} \frac{nullvalue}{aggregate+1}
\binom{nullvalue}{aggregate} \\
&= 0 - \sum_{aggregate=0}^{nullvalue} (-1)^{nullvalue-aggregate} \frac{nullvalue}{nullvalue+1} \binom{nullvalue+1}{aggregate+1} \\
&= (-1)^{nullvalue+1} \frac{nullvalue}{nullvalue+1}.
\end{aligned}
\end{multline*}

\textbf{Third solution:}
(by Richard Stanley)
The \emph{cycle indicator} of the symmetric group $singularity$ is defined by
\[
acyclicfn(lastvalue, \dots, initialval) = \sum_{staticmap \in singularity} lastvalue^{linearity(staticmap)} \cdots initialval^{linearity(staticmap)},
\]
where $linearity(staticmap)$ is the number of cycles of $staticmap$ of length $aggregate$.
Put
\[
negationsum = \sum_{staticmap \in singularity} magnitude(staticmap) constant^{mobility(staticmap)} =
acyclicfn(constant,-1,1,-1,1,\dots)
\]
and
\[
variable(nullvalue) =  \sum_{staticmap \in singularity} \frac{magnitude(staticmap)}{mobility(staticmap) + 1}
= \int_0^1 negationsum(constant)\,dconstant.
\]
A standard argument in enumerative combinatorics (the
Exponential Formula) gives
\[
\sum_{nullvalue=0}^\infty acyclicfn(lastvalue,\dots,initialval) \frac{staticvar^{nullvalue}}{nullvalue!}
= \exp \sum_{entirety=1}^{\infty} fixvalue \frac{staticvar^{entirety}}{entirety},
\]
yielding
\begin{align*}
\sum_{nullvalue=0}^\infty
variable(nullvalue) \frac{staticvar^{nullvalue}}{nullvalue!} &= \int_0^1 \exp \left( constant staticvar - \frac{staticvar^2}{2}
+ \frac{staticvar^3}{3} - \cdots \right)\,dconstant \\
&= \int_0^1 e^{(constant-1)staticvar + \log(1+staticvar)}\,dconstant \\
&= \int_0^1 (1+staticvar) e^{(constant-1)staticvar}\,dconstant \\
&= \frac{1}{staticvar} (1-e^{-staticvar}) (1+staticvar).
\end{align*}
Expanding the right side as a Taylor series and comparing coefficients
yields the desired result.

\textbf{Fourth solution (sketch):}
(by David Savitt)
We prove the identity of rational functions
\[
\sum_{staticmap \in singularity} \frac{magnitude(staticmap)}{mobility(staticmap) + constant}
= \frac{(-1)^{nullvalue+1} nullvalue! (constant+nullvalue-1)}{constant(constant+1)\cdots(constant+nullvalue)}
\]
by induction on $nullvalue$, which for $constant=1$ implies the desired result.
(This can also be deduced as in the other solutions, but in this argument
it is necessary to formulate the strong induction hypothesis.)

Let $R(nullvalue,constant)$ be the right hand side of the above equation. It is
easy to verify that
\begin{align*}
R(constant,nullvalue) &= R(constant+1,nullvalue-1) + (nullvalue-1)! \frac{(-1)^{nullvalue+1}}{constant} \\
&\qquad + \sum_{l=2}^{nullvalue-1} (-1)^{l-1}  \frac{(nullvalue-1)!}{(nullvalue-l)!} R(constant,nullvalue-l),
\end{align*}
since the sum telescopes. To prove the desired equality,
it suffices to show that the left hand side satisfies the same
recurrence. This follows because we can classify each $staticmap \in singularity$
as either fixing $nullvalue$, being an $nullvalue$-cycle, or having $nullvalue$ in an
$l$-cycle for one of $l=2,\dots,nullvalue-1$; writing the sum
over these classes gives the desired recurrence.

garbled_string.question:
Let $bcsqodjv$ denote the set of all permutations of the numbers $1,2,\dots,gplmyrwo$.
For $mxrslfgh \in bcsqodjv$, let $qzrnvxle(mxrslfgh) = 1$ if $mxrslfgh$ is an even permutation
and $qzrnvxle(mxrslfgh) = -1$ if $mxrslfgh$ is an odd permutation.
Also, let $hbcejkta(mxrslfgh)$ denote the number of fixed points of $mxrslfgh$.
Show that
\[
\sum_{mxrslfgh \in bcsqodjv} \frac{qzrnvxle(mxrslfgh)}{hbcejkta(mxrslfgh) + 1} = (-1)^{gplmyrwo+1}
\frac{gplmyrwo}{gplmyrwo+1}.
\]
garbled_string.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
Let $spqdnfhe$ be the identity matrix, and let
$kzbxpwlo$ be the matrix with $zdvmpqen$'s on the diagonal and 1's elsewhere.
Note that $kzbxpwlo - (zdvmpqen-1)spqdnfhe$, being the all 1's matrix, has rank 1 and trace $gplmyrwo$,
so has $gplmyrwo-1$ eigenvalues equal to 0 and one equal to $gplmyrwo$.
Hence $kzbxpwlo$ has $gplmyrwo-1$ eigenvalues equal to $zdvmpqen-1$ and one equal to $zdvmpqen+gplmyrwo-1$,
implying
\[
\det kzbxpwlo = (zdvmpqen+gplmyrwo-1)(zdvmpqen-1)^{gplmyrwo-1}.
\]
On the other hand, we may expand the determinant as a sum indexed by
permutations, in which case we get
\[
\det kzbxpwlo = \sum_{mxrslfgh \in bcsqodjv} \sgn(mxrslfgh) zdvmpqen^{hbcejkta(mxrslfgh)}.
\]
Integrating both sides from 0 to 1 (and substituting $ohfznkae=1-zdvmpqen$) yields
\begin{align*}
\sum_{mxrslfgh \in bcsqodjv} \frac{\sgn(mxrslfgh)}{hbcejkta(mxrslfgh) + 1}
&= \int_0^1 (zdvmpqen+gplmyrwo-1)(zdvmpqen-1)^{gplmyrwo-1}\,dzdvmpqen \\
&= \int_0^1 (-1)^{gplmyrwo+1} (gplmyrwo-ohfznkae)ohfznkae^{gplmyrwo-1}\,dohfznkae \\
&= (-1)^{gplmyrwo+1} \frac{gplmyrwo}{gplmyrwo+1},
\end{align*}
as desired.

\textbf{Second solution:}
We start by recalling a form of the principle of inclusion-exclusion:
if $raqglwzp$ is a function on the power set of $\{1, \dots, gplmyrwo\}$, then
\[
raqglwzp(S) = \sum_{gdrbmpxe \supseteq S} (-1)^{|gdrbmpxe|-|S|} \sum_{vbkslqud \supseteq gdrbmpxe} raqglwzp(vbkslqud).
\]
In this case we take $raqglwzp(S)$ to be the sum of $qzrnvxle(mxrslfgh)$
over all permutations $mxrslfgh$ whose fixed points are exactly $S$.
Then $\sum_{vbkslqud \supseteq gdrbmpxe} raqglwzp(vbkslqud) = 1$ if $|gdrbmpxe| \ge gplmyrwo-1$
and 0 otherwise (since a permutation group on 2 or more symbols has as many even and odd permutations), so
\[
raqglwzp(S) = (-1)^{gplmyrwo-|S|}(1 - gplmyrwo + |S|).
\]
The desired sum can thus be written, by grouping over fixed point sets, as
\begin{multline*}
\sum_{tyclspuv=0}^{gplmyrwo} \binom{gplmyrwo}{tyclspuv} (-1)^{gplmyrwo-tyclspuv} \frac{1-gplmyrwo+tyclspuv}{tyclspuv+1} \\
\begin{aligned}
&= \sum_{tyclspuv=0}^{gplmyrwo} (-1)^{gplmyrwo-tyclspuv} \binom{gplmyrwo}{tyclspuv} - \sum_{tyclspuv=0}^{gplmyrwo} (-1)^{gplmyrwo-tyclspuv} \frac{gplmyrwo}{tyclspuv+1}
\binom{gplmyrwo}{tyclspuv} \\
&= 0 - \sum_{tyclspuv=0}^{gplmyrwo} (-1)^{gplmyrwo-tyclspuv} \frac{gplmyrwo}{gplmyrwo+1} \binom{gplmyrwo+1}{tyclspuv+1} \\
&= (-1)^{gplmyrwo+1} \frac{gplmyrwo}{gplmyrwo+1}.
\end{aligned}
\end{multline*}

\textbf{Third solution:}
(by Richard Stanley)
The \emph{cycle indicator} of the symmetric group $bcsqodjv$ is defined by
\[
nhvclruz(umqazplk, \dots, wparklhz) = \sum_{mxrslfgh \in bcsqodjv} umqazplk^{c_1(mxrslfgh)} \cdots wparklhz^{c_{gplmyrwo}(mxrslfgh)},
\]
where $c_j(mxrslfgh)$ is the number of cycles of $mxrslfgh$ of length $j$.
Put
\[
dfqsmnty = \sum_{mxrslfgh \in bcsqodjv} qzrnvxle(mxrslfgh) zdvmpqen^{hbcejkta(mxrslfgh)} =
nhvclruz(zdvmpqen,-1,1,-1,1,\dots)
\]
and
\[
raqglwzp(gplmyrwo) =  \sum_{mxrslfgh \in bcsqodjv} \frac{qzrnvxle(mxrslfgh)}{hbcejkta(mxrslfgh) + 1}
= \int_0^1 dfqsmnty(zdvmpqen)\,dzdvmpqen.
\]
A standard argument in enumerative combinatorics (the
Exponential Formula) gives
\[
\sum_{gplmyrwo=0}^\infty nhvclruz(umqazplk,\dots,wparklhz) \frac{cipknoev^{gplmyrwo}}{gplmyrwo!}
= \exp \sum_{mxqeropd=1}^{\infty} zosymbgn \frac{cipknoev^{mxqeropd}}{mxqeropd},
\]
yielding
\begin{align*}
\sum_{gplmyrwo=0}^\infty
raqglwzp(gplmyrwo) \frac{cipknoev^{gplmyrwo}}{gplmyrwo!} &= \int_0^1 \exp \left( zdvmpqen cipknoev - \frac{cipknoev^2}{2}
+ \frac{cipknoev^3}{3} - \cdots \right)\,dzdvmpqen \\
&= \int_0^1 e^{(zdvmpqen-1)cipknoev + \log(1+cipknoev)}\,dzdvmpqen \\
&= \int_0^1 (1+cipknoev) e^{(zdvmpqen-1)cipknoev}\,dzdvmpqen \\
&= \frac{1}{cipknoev} (1-e^{-cipknoev}) (1+cipknoev).
\end{align*}
Expanding the right side as a Taylor series and comparing coefficients
yields the desired result.

\textbf{Fourth solution (sketch):}
(by David Savitt)
We prove the identity of rational functions
\[
\sum_{mxrslfgh \in bcsqodjv} \frac{qzrnvxle(mxrslfgh)}{hbcejkta(mxrslfgh) + zdvmpqen}
= \frac{(-1)^{gplmyrwo+1} gplmyrwo! (zdvmpqen+gplmyrwo-1)}{zdvmpqen(zdvmpqen+1)\cdots(zdvmpqen+gplmyrwo)}
\]
by induction on $gplmyrwo$, which for $zdvmpqen=1$ implies the desired result.
(This can also be deduced as in the other solutions, but in this argument
it is necessary to formulate the strong induction hypothesis.)

Let $R(gplmyrwo,zdvmpqen)$ be the right hand side of the above equation. It is
easy to verify that
\begin{align*}
R(gplmyrwo,zdvmpqen) &= R(zdvmpqen+1,gplmyrwo-1) + (gplmyrwo-1)! \frac{(-1)^{gplmyrwo+1}}{zdvmpqen} \\
&\qquad + \sum_{l=2}^{gplmyrwo-1} (-1)^{l-1}  \frac{(gplmyrwo-1)!}{(gplmyrwo-l)!} R(zdvmpqen,gplmyrwo-l),
\end{align*}
since the sum telescopes. To prove the desired equality,
it suffices to show that the left hand side satisfies the same
recurrence. This follows because we can classify each $mxrslfgh \in bcsqodjv$
as either fixing $gplmyrwo$, being a $gplmyrwo$-cycle, or having $gplmyrwo$ in an
$l$-cycle for one of $l=2,\dots,gplmyrwo-1$; writing the sum
over these classes gives the desired recurrence.

kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we fix $q\in\mathbb C\setminus\{0\}$ and write  
\[
S_{n}(q):=\sum_{\pi\in S_{n}}\frac{\sigma(\pi)\,q^{\,n-\nu(\pi)}}{\nu(\pi)+1}.
\]

Step 1 - Encoding the weight via the cycle index  
For $\pi\in S_{n}$ let $c_{k}(\pi)$ be the number of $k$-cycles of $\pi$.  Then  
\[
\sigma(\pi)=\prod_{k\ge 1}\bigl(-1\bigr)^{(k-1)c_{k}(\pi)},
\qquad
n-\nu(\pi)=\sum_{k\ge 2}k\,c_{k}(\pi).
\]
Hence  
\[
\sigma(\pi)\,q^{\,n-\nu(\pi)}
=
\prod_{k\ge 1}x_{k}^{c_{k}(\pi)}
\quad\text{with}\quad
x_{1}=x,\; x_{k}=(-1)^{\,k-1}q^{k}\;(k\ge 2),
\]
where the auxiliary variable $x$ keeps track of the fixed points.  
Define
\[
F_{n}(q,x):=\sum_{\pi\in S_{n}}\sigma(\pi)\,q^{\,n-\nu(\pi)}x^{\nu(\pi)}
             =Z_{n}\bigl(x_{1},x_{2},\dots,x_{n}\bigr),
\]
the cycle index $Z_{n}$ specialised at the sequence $(x_{k})$ above.

Step 2 - An exponential generating function in $t$  
The classical exponential formula gives
\[
\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}F_{n}(q,x)\frac{t^{n}}{n!}
=\exp\!\left(\sum_{k\ge 1}x_{k}\frac{t^{k}}{k}\right).
\]
With $x_{1}=x$, $x_{k}=(-1)^{\,k-1}q^{k}$ $(k\ge 2)$,  
\[
\sum_{k\ge 1}x_{k}\frac{t^{k}}{k}
=
x t + \sum_{k\ge 2}(-1)^{k-1}q^{k}\frac{t^{k}}{k}
=
x t +\log(1+q t)-q t ,
\]
because $\sum_{k\ge 1}(-1)^{k-1}(q t)^{k}/k=\log(1+q t)$.  Hence
\[
\boxed{\;
\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}F_{n}(q,x)\frac{t^{n}}{n!}
=(1+q t)\,e^{(x-q)t}}
\tag{1}
\]

Step 3 - Integration with respect to $x$  
Since $\displaystyle\int_{0}^{1}x^{\nu}\,dx=\frac{1}{\nu+1}$,  
\[
S_{n}(q)=\int_{0}^{1}F_{n}(q,x)\,dx .
\]
Define  
\[
G_{q}(t):=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}S_{n}(q)\frac{t^{n}}{n!}.
\]
Integrating (1) termwise (legitimate because all series involved have infinite radius of convergence) yields
\[
\begin{aligned}
G_{q}(t)
&=\int_{0}^{1}(1+q t)\,e^{(x-q)t}\,dx\\
&=(1+q t)\,e^{-q t}\int_{0}^{1}e^{x t}\,dx\\
&=(1+q t)\,e^{-q t}\,\frac{e^{t}-1}{t}\\
&=\frac{(1+q t)\bigl(e^{(1-q)t}-e^{-q t}\bigr)}{t}.
\end{aligned}
\]
This is precisely identity (B).

Step 4 - Extraction of the coefficient of $t^{n}$  
Expand
\[
e^{(1-q)t}-e^{-q t}
=\sum_{k\ge 0}\frac{(1-q)^{k}-( -q)^{k}}{k!}\,t^{k}.
\]
Thus  
\[
G_{q}(t)
=(1+q t)\sum_{k\ge 0}\frac{(1-q)^{k}-(-q)^{k}}{k!}\,t^{\,k-1}.
\]
Separate the contributions of $1$ and $q t$:

(i) The constant $1$ contributes the term with $k=n+1$:
\[
\frac{(1-q)^{n+1}-(-q)^{n+1}}{(n+1)!}.
\]

(ii) The factor $q t$ contributes the term with $k=n$:
\[
q\,\frac{(1-q)^{n}-(-q)^{n}}{n!}.
\]

Combining and multiplying by $(n!)$ (since $[t^{n}]G_{q}(t)=S_{n}(q)/n!$) we obtain
\[
\boxed{\;
S_{n}(q)=\frac{(1-q)^{n}(q n+1)-(-q)^{n}\,q n}{n+1}}
\tag{2}
\]
which is identity (A).

Step 5 - Sanity check $q=2$  
Putting $q=2$ in (2) gives  
\[
S_{n}(2)=(-1)^{n+1}\,\frac{2^{\,n+1}n-2n-1}{n+1},
\]
the formula stated in the kernel variant, confirming consistency.

Hence both parts (A) and (B) are proved rigorously.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Two free variables instead of one:   
  The weight now involves an arbitrary complex parameter q, so every step
  (cycle-index specialisation, exponential formula, coefficient
  extraction) must be carried out symbolically rather than with a single
  numeric substitution (q=2 in the kernel variant).  

• Full generating function required:  
  Proving the closed form for all n is no longer sufficient; the solver
  must also derive the entire exponential generating function (part B),
  demanding mastery of analytic techniques and series manipulations
  beyond those used in the original solutions.

• Heavier algebraic manipulation:  
  Handling the two competing exponentials e^{(1-q)t} and e^{–q t} and
  reorganising their difference into explicit coefficients introduces an
  extra layer of technical algebra absent from the kernel variant.

• Broader conceptual scope:  
  The solution makes essential use of the cycle index, the exponential
  formula, integration of exponential generating functions, and careful
  coefficient extraction—four distinct advanced tools that must now be
  orchestrated simultaneously.

These additions raise the problem well above the difficulty of both the
original and the kernel variant while preserving the central theme of
sign-weighted permutation statistics.

original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we fix $q\in\mathbb C\setminus\{0\}$ and write  
\[
S_{n}(q):=\sum_{\pi\in S_{n}}\frac{\sigma(\pi)\,q^{\,n-\nu(\pi)}}{\nu(\pi)+1}.
\]

Step 1 - Encoding the weight via the cycle index  
For $\pi\in S_{n}$ let $c_{k}(\pi)$ be the number of $k$-cycles of $\pi$.  Then  
\[
\sigma(\pi)=\prod_{k\ge 1}\bigl(-1\bigr)^{(k-1)c_{k}(\pi)},
\qquad
n-\nu(\pi)=\sum_{k\ge 2}k\,c_{k}(\pi).
\]
Hence  
\[
\sigma(\pi)\,q^{\,n-\nu(\pi)}
=
\prod_{k\ge 1}x_{k}^{c_{k}(\pi)}
\quad\text{with}\quad
x_{1}=x,\; x_{k}=(-1)^{\,k-1}q^{k}\;(k\ge 2),
\]
where the auxiliary variable $x$ keeps track of the fixed points.  
Define
\[
F_{n}(q,x):=\sum_{\pi\in S_{n}}\sigma(\pi)\,q^{\,n-\nu(\pi)}x^{\nu(\pi)}
             =Z_{n}\bigl(x_{1},x_{2},\dots,x_{n}\bigr),
\]
the cycle index $Z_{n}$ specialised at the sequence $(x_{k})$ above.

Step 2 - An exponential generating function in $t$  
The classical exponential formula gives
\[
\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}F_{n}(q,x)\frac{t^{n}}{n!}
=\exp\!\left(\sum_{k\ge 1}x_{k}\frac{t^{k}}{k}\right).
\]
With $x_{1}=x$, $x_{k}=(-1)^{\,k-1}q^{k}$ $(k\ge 2)$,  
\[
\sum_{k\ge 1}x_{k}\frac{t^{k}}{k}
=
x t + \sum_{k\ge 2}(-1)^{k-1}q^{k}\frac{t^{k}}{k}
=
x t +\log(1+q t)-q t ,
\]
because $\sum_{k\ge 1}(-1)^{k-1}(q t)^{k}/k=\log(1+q t)$.  Hence
\[
\boxed{\;
\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}F_{n}(q,x)\frac{t^{n}}{n!}
=(1+q t)\,e^{(x-q)t}}
\tag{1}
\]

Step 3 - Integration with respect to $x$  
Since $\displaystyle\int_{0}^{1}x^{\nu}\,dx=\frac{1}{\nu+1}$,  
\[
S_{n}(q)=\int_{0}^{1}F_{n}(q,x)\,dx .
\]
Define  
\[
G_{q}(t):=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}S_{n}(q)\frac{t^{n}}{n!}.
\]
Integrating (1) termwise (legitimate because all series involved have infinite radius of convergence) yields
\[
\begin{aligned}
G_{q}(t)
&=\int_{0}^{1}(1+q t)\,e^{(x-q)t}\,dx\\
&=(1+q t)\,e^{-q t}\int_{0}^{1}e^{x t}\,dx\\
&=(1+q t)\,e^{-q t}\,\frac{e^{t}-1}{t}\\
&=\frac{(1+q t)\bigl(e^{(1-q)t}-e^{-q t}\bigr)}{t}.
\end{aligned}
\]
This is precisely identity (B).

Step 4 - Extraction of the coefficient of $t^{n}$  
Expand
\[
e^{(1-q)t}-e^{-q t}
=\sum_{k\ge 0}\frac{(1-q)^{k}-( -q)^{k}}{k!}\,t^{k}.
\]
Thus  
\[
G_{q}(t)
=(1+q t)\sum_{k\ge 0}\frac{(1-q)^{k}-(-q)^{k}}{k!}\,t^{\,k-1}.
\]
Separate the contributions of $1$ and $q t$:

(i) The constant $1$ contributes the term with $k=n+1$:
\[
\frac{(1-q)^{n+1}-(-q)^{n+1}}{(n+1)!}.
\]

(ii) The factor $q t$ contributes the term with $k=n$:
\[
q\,\frac{(1-q)^{n}-(-q)^{n}}{n!}.
\]

Combining and multiplying by $(n!)$ (since $[t^{n}]G_{q}(t)=S_{n}(q)/n!$) we obtain
\[
\boxed{\;
S_{n}(q)=\frac{(1-q)^{n}(q n+1)-(-q)^{n}\,q n}{n+1}}
\tag{2}
\]
which is identity (A).

Step 5 - Sanity check $q=2$  
Putting $q=2$ in (2) gives  
\[
S_{n}(2)=(-1)^{n+1}\,\frac{2^{\,n+1}n-2n-1}{n+1},
\]
the formula stated in the kernel variant, confirming consistency.

Hence both parts (A) and (B) are proved rigorously.
